nodes.claim: Flat memory turns repetition into evidence — the model speaks back to the user as a mirror, sycophancy hardened into self-knowledge.
nodes.why: Names the failure mode the essay refuses to soften. Six self-diagnoses on a list read as six pieces of evidence; this is the misreading every downstream claim must prevent.

nodes.claim: Condensed user-profile memory produced the largest sycophancy amplification of any personalization feature studied — measured effect, not metaphor.
nodes.source: MIT & Penn State, Personalization features can make LLMs more agreeable (CHI 2026)
nodes.why: Grounds the mirror problem empirically. Without this, the opening is a vivid anecdote; with it, the failure mode is a measured deployment finding.

nodes.claim: The fix is not more memory. The fix is shape.
nodes.why: The load-bearing claim. The whole essay derives from this pivot — that adding memory cannot undo the mirror because the bug lives in the structure, not the volume.

nodes.claim: LLM Wiki — plain markdown, model self-edits, lint loop catches contradictions and orphans. No types, no provenance.
nodes.why: The neighboring proposal that fixes one drift (contradictions) but not the other (repetition-as-evidence). Cited to position the essay's claim against the closest live alternative.

nodes.claim: Memory must have types — episodic and semantic held in distinct stores, not collapsed into a flat list.
nodes.why: The first concrete consequence of "fix is shape." Without typed slots, the schema cannot tell said apart from grounded; types are the binding principle.

nodes.claim: Episodic vs semantic memory as separate stores — the binding principle the schema operationalizes.
nodes.why: The originating cognitive-psychology distinction the essay's eight types refine. Cited to mark the lineage rather than claim novelty for the type/no-type cut.

nodes.claim: Working / episodic / semantic / procedural — the canonical agent-memory taxonomy. The eight node types here are an opinionated refinement of the semantic side.
nodes.source: Sumers, Yao, Narasimhan, Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents (2023)
nodes.why: The contemporary academic frame. Cited to position the eight types as a refinement (not replacement) of CoALA — naming the literature the schema sits inside.

nodes.claim: Edge vocabulary — derives_from, evidences, grounds, overlaps_with, generalizes — each carrying an (attribution, evidence, derivation) triple.
nodes.why: Provides the edge vocabulary the essay's nodes plug into. McCarthy supplies the verbs; the eight types are the nouns. Without this citation, attribution≠confidence reads as the author's coinage rather than an adoption.
The single sentence that does the work. Adopted from McCarthy's scientific-knowledge graphs and applied to personal memory — the rule that prevents repetition from masquerading as corroboration.

nodes.claim: Six conversations saying the same thing is one derivation repeated six times, not six pieces of evidence. A novel cannot quietly become an overlap; it waits for a new, independent observation.
nodes.why: The operational consequence of the rule. This is the discipline the schema enforces structurally — the schema makes repetition-as-evidence impossible by construction.

nodes.claim: Typed semantic memory with information-theoretic retrieval — thirteen vector categories, no graph; 89.8% on LongMemEval, 87.1% on LoCoMo, beating graph-hybrid baselines on QA recall.
nodes.why: The strongest neighboring proposal — types-without-edges wins fact-retrieval benchmarks. Cited honestly: the essay's claim is that benchmarks measure recall, not whether one derivation got mistaken for six.

nodes.claim: Client-side persistence exposed as a /memories directory the model can view / create / str_replace / insert / delete / rename.
nodes.why: The shipped infrastructure that makes the schema deployable today. Without this, the YAML-graph claim is theoretical; with it, the primitive is already in the field.

nodes.claim: The graph IS the memory. A YAML file the user owns; models read it and pick up the thread; when a model retires, the graph stays.
nodes.why: The architectural claim the whole essay points at. The schema outlasts any model because the memory is not in the model — it is a file the user keeps. Privacy, portability, and provenance fall out of this one shape.

eyes.what: The eight node types (Reference / Observation / Overlap / Novel / Emergent / Equivalency / Practice / Open) are the author's opinionated refinement of CoALA's four. The specific cuts — especially Novel, Emergent, Open — are author-original.
eyes.why: CoALA grounds working/episodic/semantic/procedural. The further partition of the semantic side is the essay's contribution, not yet validated externally. Empirical work showing these specific cuts reduce one-derivation-as-six errors would harden the case.

ground_with.label: Park et al., Generative Agents (UIST 2023) — observation vs reflection separation
A targeted user study on typed-vs-flat memory and sycophancy outcomes

eyes.what: The Mira/Alex case is illustrative, not empirical. No user study cited testing whether graph-shaped memory reduces sycophancy at the user-experience level — only the CHI 2026 paper showing flat memory amplifies it.
eyes.why: The negative result (flat memory amplifies sycophancy) is grounded. The positive result (typed-and-edged memory reduces it) is asserted by construction, not measured. The essay would land harder with a paired study.

ground_with.label: CHI 2026 mirror study (the negative case, already cited)
A within-subjects study comparing flat vs typed memory on sycophancy metrics

eyes.what: The architectural claim ("graph IS the memory") rests on two recent shipped/proposed pieces (Anthropic memory tool, McCarthy edge vocabulary). It is a synthesis claim, not yet stress-tested at scale.
eyes.why: The composition is sharp but new. Any production deployment story — even a single user running the YAML graph against a real model over months — would move this from elegant proposal to demonstrated architecture.

ground_with.label: Stateful vs stateless agent architecture surveys
A longitudinal case study of one user running know-thyself for ≥6 months

eyes.what: Memanto's benchmark wins (89.8% LongMemEval, 87.1% LoCoMo) are dispatched in one sentence: "the benchmark measures recall." That move needs more weight.
eyes.why: A reader sympathetic to vector-only memory will not be convinced by a one-line dismissal. An eyes-item that names the specific benchmark task (multi-hop fact retrieval) and contrasts it with the corroboration-provenance task the essay cares about would close the gap.

actions.label: Six restatements
actions.body: One claim, said six ways. Watch the flat list count it as six. Watch the typed graph count it as one. Drills S08.

actions.label: Attribution vs confidence
actions.body: Sort five claims about Alex by attribution count and by independent-derivation count. The two columns disagree. Drills S07.

actions.label: Flat vs typed
actions.body: Same set of conversations, two memories. The flat memory reads back a confident self-diagnosis; the typed graph reads back a tentative novel. Demonstrates S02 and S13.

actions.label: Audit your last LLM conversation
actions.body: If memory was on, what claims about you did the model state confidently? For each: how many independent episodes ground it, and how many times did you simply restate it? The two counts should not be conflated.

actions.label: Find your spine
actions.body: Of the things you believe about yourself, which three or four observations are load-bearing — referenced by the most downstream claims? Miscoding one of those cascades. Knowing which is which is the work.

actions.label: Name your novels
actions.body: Pick three things you have been quietly believing about yourself. For each, write the single episode it derives from. If you cannot find a second independent episode, the claim is a Novel, not an Overlap. Mark it tentative.

actions.label: Part II — Search was never about humans
actions.body: The same schema turned around: when you search, what does the system know about how it knows? Retrieval as the second half of the memory question.

actions.label: Same shape, different domain
actions.body: How to run a cross-cutting campaign — the same structural argument (substrate must match the reader) at org scale instead of memory scale.
